description: Causal + structural rules for digital synthesis stage (Yosys+ABC, ORFS, DC, Genus) — area/cell-distribution anchors, WNS predictors, warning gates, and PPA tradeoffs.

synth.total_cell_area_um2.description: Sum of stdcell areas after synth (computed by oracle from gate_counts × liberty).

synth.total_cells.description: Total mapped cell count from yosys stat.rpt.

synth.top_module_by_cells.description: Name of the leaf module with the largest mapped cell count.

synth.module_ranking.description: Top-N modules by cell count, each with module/total_cells/comb_cells/ff_cells.

synth.ff_share_pct.description: FF cells as a percentage of total mapped cells.

synth.invbuf_share_pct.description: INV+BUF cell types as a percentage of total mapped cells after ABC mapping.

synth.icg_per_1kff.description: Integrated clock-gating cells inserted per 1000 FFs.

synth.warnings.latch.description: Yosys/Verilator latch-inference warning count after elaboration.

synth.warnings.width.description: Width / truncation warning count from Verilator/Yosys.

synth.warnings.multidriven.description: Multidriven-net warning count from Yosys check / Verilator.

synth.wns_ns.description: Worst Negative Slack reported at synth-stage STA (ABC -D / opensta on synth netlist).

synth.runtime_s.description: Wall-clock runtime of the synth flow (yosys+abc) in seconds.

synth.mul_latency_cycles.description: Architectural latency in cycles for the multiplier instruction.

synth.mapping_engine.description: Which ABC variant was used for tech mapping (abc | abc9 | abc_new).

synth.critical_path_block.description: Module/cone label that owns the synth-stage critical path.

synth.regfile_storage_floor_um2.description: Lower-bound area of the architectural register-file storage bits alone (FF count × DFF cell area).

synth.area_delta_pct_vs_fast_mult.description: Percent delta in total stdcell area versus an Ibex-RV32MFast configuration baseline.

synth.area_delta_pct_vs_single_cycle.description: Percent delta in total stdcell area versus an Ibex single-cycle multiplier configuration baseline.

pnr.utilization_pct.description: Core utilization after floorplan / global placement.

pnr.congestion_h_pct.description: Horizontal congestion overflow percentage from global route.

sta.wns_ps.description: Worst Negative Slack at signoff STA.

sta.failing_endpoints.description: Count of failing endpoints at signoff STA.

sta.hold_violations.description: Count of hold-time violations at signoff STA.

rules.statement: For an Ibex-class small RV32 core with M-extension, FF register file, and open-library sky130hd mapping, post-synth stdcell area lands in a tight band (~148k um^2) that is a useful pre-floorplan sanity anchor.

prevents.mechanism: A calibrated synth-area expectation lets PnR pick die size correctly the first time, avoiding a re-floorplan loop.

rules.rationale: ORFS publishes maintained per-PDK Ibex rule files with these area numbers, and the sky130hd ORFS Ibex tutorial reports ~191k um^2 post-flow on a 796x798 core, directionally consistent once buffering and placement overhead are added.

rules.statement: For an Ibex-class small RV32 core with M-extension, FF register file, and open-library nangate45 mapping, post-synth stdcell area lands in a tight band (~32.5k um^2) that is a useful pre-floorplan sanity anchor.

prevents.mechanism: A calibrated synth-area expectation lets PnR pick die size correctly the first time, avoiding a re-floorplan loop.

rules.rationale: ORFS publishes maintained per-PDK Ibex rule files with these area numbers, and the sky130hd ORFS Ibex tutorial reports ~191k um^2 post-flow on a 796x798 core, directionally consistent once buffering and placement overhead are added.

rules.statement: For an Ibex-class small RV32 core with M-extension, FF register file, and open-library asap7 mapping, post-synth stdcell area lands in a tight band (~2.43k um^2) that is a useful pre-floorplan sanity anchor.

prevents.mechanism: A calibrated synth-area expectation lets PnR pick die size correctly the first time, avoiding a re-floorplan loop.

rules.rationale: ORFS publishes maintained per-PDK Ibex rule files with these area numbers, and the sky130hd ORFS Ibex tutorial reports ~191k um^2 post-flow on a 796x798 core, directionally consistent once buffering and placement overhead are added.

rules.statement: In Ibex-class cores, switching to a single-cycle multiplier is usually the largest single architectural area step at synth: about +15% area at 100 MHz and +33% at max frequency, because three 17x17 multipliers are instantiated in parallel.

prevents.mechanism: Knowing the multiplier is the dominant contributor lets the floorplanner reserve area and avoid a congestion-driven RTL rework.

rules.rationale: CARRV measured +15-33% area for SC-Mult while max frequency was barely affected; the Ibex docs explicitly say SC-Mult costs ~3-4x the fast multiplier's area because three parallel 17x17 multipliers are instantiated.

rules.statement: Fast/slow multicycle multipliers are the area-efficient default for ASIC-like open flows: RV32MFast lands at 3-4 cycles, RV32MSlow stretches MUL to clog2(op_b)+1 cycles and MULH to 33 cycles, trading latency for a clearly smaller block than the single-cycle option.

prevents.mechanism: Picking the multicycle variant up front avoids chasing single-cycle RV32M for free performance and paying for it in area + congestion later.

rules.rationale: Public Ibex docs and CARRV measurements agree: the architectural knob changes area far more reliably than it improves the achievable clock in small in-order cores.

rules.statement: A 31x32 FF register file carries a large storage floor before read-mux logic is counted: ~24.8k um^2 in sky130hd, ~4.5k um^2 in Nangate45, ~0.29k um^2 in ASAP7 from public DFF cell areas alone.

prevents.mechanism: Recognizing the regfile floor stops engineers from misattributing core area to control logic and chasing the wrong target during floorplan optimization.

rules.rationale: The Ibex docs steer ASIC users to latch-based storage and FPGA users to RAM-backed storage precisely because the FF storage decision dominates area early; the floor is computed from actual published DFF cell areas across three PDKs.

rules.statement: For mux/steer-dominated bus fabrics at fixed topology, doubling data width pushes synth cell count by roughly 1.7-2.3x, because steering logic remains largely bit-sliced even after Yosys mux reduction.

prevents.mechanism: Treating a 32->64 bit fabric bump as 'just wires' creates surprise congestion at PnR; this rule flags the synth-area expectation early.

rules.rationale: Yosys' mux-reduction passes and Basilisk's part-select postmortem agree that mux structure and width-dependent selection lowering are first-order QoR determinants in bit-sliced fabrics.

rules.statement: In DSP-style integer datapaths, adders scale near-linearly with width while multiplier cost and delay grow superlinearly; pipelining or decomposition mostly shifts the area-delay Pareto rather than removing the multiplier as the dominant block.

prevents.mechanism: Targeting multiplier optimization (rather than adders) before STA avoids burning effort on a path that is not the limiter.

rules.rationale: The 2024 multiplier study reports new Pareto points from decomposition/pipelining but no elimination of the fundamental tradeoff; OpenROAD ALU experiments show similar relative behavior across sky130hd and ASAP7.

rules.statement: In a small Ibex-class core with FF regfile, the FF share of total mapped cells lands in the low teens to ~20%; the regfile alone accounts for ~12-17% of synth area on the public ORFS Ibex datasets before pipeline+CSR flops are added.

prevents.mechanism: Anchoring expected sequential footprint avoids hours wasted hunting 'mystery combinational bloat' when the sequential footprint is structurally normal.

rules.rationale: This rule is anchored to actual public DFF cell areas and ORFS Ibex area budgets, not architectural intuition: 992 architectural bits map to 11.9-16.8% of synth area across the three open PDKs.

rules.statement: In an Ibex-like RV32IM core, the mult/div block is typically the top module by cells once M is enabled, because multiplier choice is the single biggest area knob and Ibex's multiplier variants instantiate large internal arithmetic structures.

prevents.mechanism: Knowing multdiv is the top leaf focuses module-level optimization on the real cell hotspot rather than decode/CSR fluff.

rules.rationale: The published area deltas for SC-Mult vs Fast vs Slow are large enough that leaf dominance by multdiv is the practical default unless a custom block obviously overrides it.

rules.statement: A noticeable INV/BUF tail after technology mapping is normal in open Yosys+ABC flows; once INV/BUF share is clearly into double digits pre-PnR, treat it as a fanout/timing pressure signal worth inspection rather than harmless noise.

prevents.mechanism: Catching an INV/BUF spike at synth flags fanout/timing pressure before it pushes congestion into PnR.

rules.rationale: Yosys' ABC and dfflibmap flows can insert buffers and inverters under constrained mapping; ORFS exposes REMOVE_ABC_BUFFERS as a dedicated knob; Basilisk shows Yosys netlist reduction creates very high-fanout nets where buffer tails stop being benign.

rules.statement: In open Yosys+ABC ASIC flows, integrated clock-gating insertion is essentially zero unless RTL or a wrapper library already instantiates ICGs — ORFS exposes them as black boxes rather than via aggressive automatic insertion.

prevents.mechanism: Recognizing that automatic ICG insertion will not save dynamic power forces explicit power-strategy work earlier, before the power surprise at signoff.

rules.rationale: This is one of the clearest practical differences from commercial synthesis: Ibex's latch regfile even requires an explicit prim_clock_gating wrapper, and ORFS does not auto-insert ICGs.

rules.statement: In Ibex-class cores with a multicycle multiplier, the worst path is more likely in control / instruction-request logic than in the multiplier itself; SBP and BT-ALU cut max frequency by 22% and 16% in CARRV while SC-Mult barely changed Fmax.

prevents.mechanism: Pointing timing effort at the control/branch cone instead of the multiplier closes WNS faster than re-architecting the arithmetic.

rules.rationale: Public Ibex evidence shows area dominance and critical-path dominance can diverge: the multiplier is the area hotspot, but the instruction-request side of the machine is the frequency limiter.

rules.statement: Turning a long arithmetic function into a multicycle path predictably reduces immediate combinational depth at the cost of CPI: Ibex Fast spreads the multiplier across 3-4 cycles and Slow up to 33 cycles, shifting the cost from WNS to architectural latency.

prevents.mechanism: Architectural staging converts a WNS problem into an extra cycle of latency, avoiding the trap of trying to rescue a too-deep arithmetic cone with sizing alone.

rules.rationale: This is stronger than a generic 'pipeline more' suggestion because the Ibex docs quantify the cycle shifts for each variant; multicycle is the lever that actually changes the timing object.

rules.statement: In open synthesis, operator chains such as adder->mux->adder are reliable WNS hazards because Yosys provides dedicated balance/reduction passes and Basilisk shows poor part-select/mux lowering can create much larger combinational cones than the RTL author intended.

prevents.mechanism: Inserting a register or restructuring the chain at synth review prevents a placement-only fix attempt later.

rules.rationale: Yosys documents extra passes (opt_balance_tree, opt_reduce) specifically for these chains, which is the strongest signal that they recur often enough to deserve a dedicated review rule.

rules.statement: High-fanout control and decode nets are stronger WNS predictors than leaf size in Yosys-based flows because Yosys reduces redundant logic without considering placement, creating very high-fanout nets that ABC only partially repairs with library timing assumptions.

prevents.mechanism: Catching a high-fanout control net at synth (and partitioning or pipelining it) prevents the silent post-place WNS regression that Yosys+ABC cannot fix on its own.

rules.rationale: Basilisk's postmortem ties Yosys netlist reduction to very high-fanout nets; ORFS exposes default driver/load assumptions precisely because these electrical surrogates matter when the netlist was not built with placement awareness.

rules.statement: Flat synthesis is usually the best pure-QoR setting in Yosys because flattening removes module boundaries, but it carries the largest runtime and loses hierarchy useful for debug and physical planning.

prevents.mechanism: Choosing flat versus hierarchical deliberately stops engineers from running blind full-flat iterations and paying compile-time tax on every change.

rules.rationale: Yosys' keep_hierarchy and flatten controls exist precisely because this is a first-order QoR/runtime tradeoff, not a cosmetic one; ORFS maintainers describe the same tradeoff explicitly in their hierarchy discussion.

rules.statement: The most practical open-flow compromise is a two-pass scheme: measure module sizes hierarchically, then flatten small modules while preserving larger ones — ORFS documents this as their workaround for Yosys' difficulty in choosing keep lists automatically.

prevents.mechanism: Two-pass keeplist tuning escapes the binary 'flat or hierarchical' trap and recovers QoR that pure-hierarchy iteration leaves on the table.

rules.rationale: ORFS' published hierarchy variables (HIER_RTLMP, MIN_AREA, keep-module lists) make clear this is an active design-space knob, not an edge-case hack.

rules.statement: For standard-cell ASIC synthesis in current public Yosys flows, abc is the default and abc9 is not the baseline: upstream docs describe abc9 primarily in FPGA terms, and ORFS exposes ABC_AREA / ABC_SPEED knobs rather than an ASIC abc9 recipe.

prevents.mechanism: Stopping engineers from benchmarking abc9 by default avoids misapplied FPGA-focused assumptions and the wasted hours of 'why is QoR worse on ASIC?'

rules.rationale: The 2024 PULP talk frames open synthesis work around better control of abc and next-gen mappers, not abc9 as an already-settled ASIC solution.

rules.statement: Yosys cleanup/sharing passes (opt_clean, opt_hier, opt_share, opt_balance_tree) are local and conditional rather than magical: they trim duplicates and rebalance trees but do not invent new architectural cuts.

prevents.mechanism: Setting expectations that opt passes give incremental gains stops engineers from waiting for Yosys to fix a pipeline-depth problem and forces the architectural change earlier.

rules.rationale: Recent open-flow gap analyses still call out missing retiming/cloning, which is the clearest sign that local pass tuning has structural limits.

rules.statement: A latch warning at synth should stop normal review unless the latch is intentional, because Verilator and Yosys both treat incomplete combinational assignment as latch inference, which introduces level-sensitive timing into an otherwise edge-triggered flow.

prevents.mechanism: Catching latches at synth review prevents transparent timing paths from surfacing as confusing STA failures downstream.

rules.rationale: 'It simulates' is not an adequate defense here: the synth product is a different timing object than the intended flop-based design once a latch is inferred.

rules.statement: Width/truncation warnings are not lint cosmetics in synth review: Verilator documents them as real size mismatches, and Basilisk shows poor part-select lowering can create much larger mux structures than necessary when width-dependent semantics are mishandled.

prevents.mechanism: Fixing width mismatches at synth prevents both functional escapes and unnecessary datapath bloat from accidental width growth.

rules.rationale: Width mismatches are one of the rare warning classes that can be both a correctness and a QoR problem at the same time, which is why they deserve a hard zero-tolerance review gate.

rules.statement: A multidriven warning is almost always a bug-level event at synth review: Yosys check flags conflicting drivers as core correctness, and Verilator warns that multiple driving blocks can create CDC or timing bugs even when simulation runs.

prevents.mechanism: Halting the review on multidriven nets prevents burning cycles on QoR work before basic single-driver correctness is restored.

rules.rationale: This is one of the few warning classes where the correct response is 'halt the review' rather than 'note it and continue' — the bug-escape risk is too high.

rules.statement: Tightening target delay in open flows usually gives a convex area/runtime tradeoff: Yosys abc -D plus retiming/buffering accepts tighter periods at rising area and runtime cost, and OpenROAD's public Ibex DOE shows pushing the clock harder broadens the WNS spread while sky130hs area moves within a relatively tight 240k-280k um^2 window.

prevents.mechanism: Recognizing the convex curve stops engineers from over-interpreting one aggressive run as the new baseline and chasing unstable QoR.

rules.rationale: Even ORFS' simple gcd example only shows a modest area shift between ABC_SPEED and ABC_AREA; once the easy wins are gone, tighter timing buys runtime/buffer churn more than clean architectural improvement.

rules.statement: Yosys+ABC's default scripts include dretime, but public open-flow gap analyses still flag register retiming and cloning as missing or insufficient for complex SoCs — expect local depth smoothing rather than commercial-grade whole-chip retiming.

prevents.mechanism: Setting the right expectation for ABC retiming prevents teams from assuming the open flow has already exhausted sequential optimization options and catches escalation decisions earlier.

rules.rationale: OpenROAD's MegaBoom issue ties QoR limits to missing retiming and cloning; the PULP 2024 talk also calls out constraints and timing support as open challenges.

rules.statement: The same Ibex RTL shrinks from ~148k um^2 on sky130hd to ~32.5k on Nangate45 to ~2.43k on ASAP7 in maintained ORFS collateral; these ratios are useful first-order anchors but a poor basis for extrapolating to other advanced nodes because library architecture, routing rules, and SRAM usage differ dramatically.

prevents.mechanism: Refusing false precision in node-port estimates avoids misleading promises about 'automatic' area collapse at advanced nodes.

rules.rationale: The point of the rule is to keep the useful anchor while refusing the false certainty: open-flow studies repeatedly emphasize that QoR depends on netlist structure and physical effects rather than node label alone.
